editionStmt.edition: Digital Critical Edition of the Chapter Summaries (Capitula) to Deuteronomium in the Tours Bibles

publicationStmt.p:
Welcome to the digital critical edition project of the Capitula to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles!
The editions are part of the PRIN 2022 PNRR project DOBiPS – Data Oriented Biblical Paratext Studies, awarded for the 2023–2025 period to the research units at the Universities of Udine (P.I. E. Colombi) and Cassino (P.I. R. Casavecchia), under the competitive PRIN PNRR call – National Recovery and Resilience Plan, Mission 4 "Education and Research", funded by the European Union’s NextGenerationEU programme (protocol no. P2022ZW4AW).
The Capitula prefixed to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles have been encoded in XML/TEI and published using the software EVT (Edition Visualization Technology).
The editions were prepared by members of the Udine research unit: Emanuela Colombi, Margherita Filippozzi, and Silverio Franzoni.
Details of the encoding choices and related methodological reflections have been presented in the working paper: E. Colombi, M. Filippozzi, S. Franzoni, The Digital Critical Edition of the Capitula to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles, DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.16761173.
Information on the individual editions is available by clicking the 'info' button at the top of the left-hand pane.

bibl.author: Donatien de Bruyne
bibl.title: Sommaires, divisions et rubriques de la Bible latine

bibl.author: Bernhard Bischoff
bibl.title: Katalog der festländischen Handschriften des neunten Jahrhunderts (mit Ausnahme der wisigotischen)
Pentateuchi versio latina antiquissima e Codice Lugdunensi. Version latine du Pentateuque antérieure à saint Jérôme...
Heptateuchi partis posterioris versio Latina antiquissima e codice Lugdunensi. Version latine du Deutéronome, de Josué et des Juges antérieure à saint Jérôme...

bibl.editor: Monachi abbatiae pontificiae Sancti Hieronymi in Urbe
bibl.title: Biblia Sacra iuxta Latinam vulgatam versionem ad codicum fidem... Libros Numerorum et Deuteronomii ex interpretatione sancti Hieronymi cum variis capitulorum seriebus

listWit.witness: Text of series A as printed by D. de Bruyne, Sommaires..., p. 34-40

tagsDecl.rendition: the digital reproduction is b/w, impossible to determine color

front.p:
This digital edition presents the reconstruction of the capitula of Series A (according to Donatien de Bruyne's classification) that precede the Book of Deuteronomium, in the textual form known from the Tours scriptorium (Φ).
For an in-depth analysis of the paratexts to the Deuteronomium in Tours, Beneventan and Atlantic Bibles, see R. Casavecchia, E. Colombi, S. Franzoni, G. Macchiarelli, M. Sparagna, ...
This series comprises 155 capitula of varying length, each reproducing, often in a rather literal way, either the beginning or significant portion(s) of the corresponding passage of Deuteronomium.
As comparison between the different textual forms shows, the A series is based on a Vetus Latina version of the Deuteronomium (transmitted in a continuous form only by the Codex Lugdunensis: Lyon, BM, 403 + 1964, siglum VL 100; edition by U. Robert, 1881 and Id., 1900). See, e.g. (further details are provided in the article cited above):
cap. 2 (Dt 1.9) non potero solus sufferre uos A, VL 100 : non possum solus sustinere uos Vulg.
cap. 3 (Dt 1.19) tollentes A, VL 100 : profecti Vulg.
cap. 14 (Dt 4.23) adtendite (attendite uobis VL 100) ne obliuiscamini testamentum (testamenti VL 100) domini dei uestri A, VL 100 : caue nequando obliuiscaris pacti domini dei tui Vulg.
cap. 21 (Dt 6.15) ne eradicemini A : cf. ne... eradicet te a facie terrae VL 100 : nequando... auferat te de superficie terrae Vulg.
cap. 32 (Dt 9.8) in Choreb exacerbastis dominum A, VL 100 : in Horeb provocasti eum Vulg.
cap. 45 (Dt 10.12) postulat a te aliud (nihil aliud VL 100) quam ut A, VL 100 : petit a te nisi ut Vulg.
cap. 58 (Dt 12.32) omne uerbum quod ego mando tibi A, VL 100 : quod praecipio tibi Vulg.
cap. 72 (Dt 15.19) omne primitiuum A, VL 100 : de primogenitis Vulg.
cap. 94 (Dt 21.18) si (si autem VL 100) fuerit alicui (alicuius VL 100) filius A, VL 100 : si genuerit homo filium Vulg.
cap. 132 (Dt 25.4) non infrenabis bouem triturantem A, VL 100 : non ligabis os bouis terentis Vulg.
cap. 149 (Dt 31.7) uiriliter age et conualesce A, VL 100 : confortare et esto robustus Vulg. Although very close to the text transmitted by VL 100, the source of the A series was certainly a slightly different translation, as shown by discrepancies such as the following (further examples in the article cited above):
cap. 19 (Dt 6.5) ex toto corde tuo et ex tota uirtute tua A : ex toto corde tuo et ex tota anima tua VL 100 : ex toto corde tuo et ex tota anima tua et ex tota fortitudine tua Vulg. (cf. the Greek text of the LXX: ἐξ ὅλης τῆς καρδίας σου καὶ ἐξ ὅλης τῆς ψυχῆς σου καὶ ἐξ ὅλης τῆς δυνάμεώς σου).
cap. 58 (Dt 12.32) custodi et fac A : hoc custodi facere VL 100 : hoc tantum facito Vulg.
cap. 94 (Dt 21.18) alicui A : alicuius VL 100 (cf. LXX: τινι)
ibid. inob(o)ediens A : insuadibilis et contumax VL 100 : cf. contumacem et protervum Vulg.
cap. 107 (Dt 23.1) abscisus A : addiae et archigallus VL 100 : eunuchus adtritis vel amputatis testiculis et absciso veretro Vulg. Given this consistent proximity, it was decided not to mark explicitly, throughout the text, every agreement between the A series and the Vetus Latina against the Vulgate.
It should be noted that, in contrast to all the other Tours Bibles, P3 (Paris, BnF, lat. 47) attests de Bruyne’s B series (p. 35-41) rather than the A series (see also the article cited above).
From a philological perspective, the study of the capitula to Deuteronomium yields only limited results, since errors within the tradition are generally rare and of little significance. A more detailed analysis, however, becomes possible by comparing the Tours Bibles with two non-Alcuinian witnesses, namely the Maurdramnus Bible (Am1) and the Tours Octateuch (Tu). Such analysis is presented in the article cited above and is here summarized; further details can be found in specific notes throughout the text (see below).
No stable grouping can be established within the corpus of the Tours Bibles. However, all or most of them share certain readings that may be considered "idiosyncratic" from a linguistic point of view, against the non-Alcuinian witnesses and the text edited by de Bruyne. See, e.g.:
cap. 79 alienigena] alienigenam Sg1 P1pc K, Tu (-gin-), de Bruyne
cap. 118 turpam] turpem P1pc, Am1, Tu, de Bruyne
cap. 152 morire] morere Sg1pc, Am1, Tu, de Bruyne In the first example, Am1 shares the same ungrammatical reading, but this may be due to the large number of phonological and morphological irregularities that characterize this manuscript.
Such peculiarities may derive from an ancestor with irregular linguistic features; however, they generally find no counterpart in the text of the Codex Lugdunensis, which coincides with the Tours Bibles in only one case, odierit for oderit in Dt 22.13 / cap. 101.
The cases in which both the Alcuinian (Φ) and non-Alcuinian (Am1 and Tu) Bibles are in error may go back to a very early ancestor in the transmission of the A series, from which all of our witnesses would ultimately descend. By contrast, those in which only Φ is in error may have the same origin, although partly obscured by corrections in the ancestors of Am1 and Tu, or else be ascribed to a common ancestor of the Tours Bibles. The existence of a common ancestor of Φ, Am1 and Tu could also be suggested by two further errors:
cap. 22 dices quemadmodum de Bruyne : dicesque admodum Am1, Tu : dicesque quemadmodum Φ
cap. 85 posuerunt de Bruyne : posuerant Φ, Am1, Tu The first case may also confirm the existence of a common ancestor of the Tours Bibles, to which the (erroneous) correction of dicesque admodum into dicesque quemadmodum would be attributable.
We also find some errors shared only by Am1 and Tu, for instance decoribus for de choreb in cap. 3. It is difficult, however, to determine whether such errors are to be ascribed to a common ancestor of Am1 and Tu against Φ, or whether they go back to the hypothetical ancestor from which Am1, Tu and Φ may derive, but were subsequently corrected in Φ.
Throughout the edition, certain words are highlighted in different ways in order to signal noteworthy features. Bold type indicates adiaphoric discrepancies between Φ and the non-Alcuinian witnesses or against de Bruyne's edition; italic type highlights discrepancies where Φ's reading is grammatically incorrect.
For technical reasons, the filter tool ("Filtri" button below the text) cannot be customized by the reader, but it is permanently active in order to display certain remarkable features. In the edited text (view mode "Testo di lettura"), colors indicate the reason (the @cause attribute) for specific readings, as follows:
grammatical: pink;
orthographic: blue;
slip: red;
Vulgate: green. In each witness's text (view mode "Collazione"), the filters highlight (in brown) the points where a correction was made.
Occasional commentary on the readings is provided within the text itself (clickable red dots); further remarks can be found under the apparatus (right-hand panel of the screen "Testo di lettura"), either by clicking on "Nota critica" or by accessing the XML structure (when the information refers to a single witness and is therefore embedded in a witDetail element).
The point in the text of Deuteronomium where the witnesses place the chapter number corresponding to each capitulum can be accessed either by clicking on the two-page icon next to each capitulum number, or by clicking on the "Passi paralleli" thumbnail (in the right-hand panel of the "Testo di lettura" screen).

rdgGrp.witDetail: in the top margin, at the center of the page
CAPTULa written without i in the manuscript.

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Verba quae locutus loquutus est moyses omni israhel
omni israhel
quadragesimo xlmo anno in
heremo eremo Φ, Am1, Tu vs de Bruyne (orthography) post occisionem seon et og regum per omnia quae praecepit praecipit prȩcepit The trivial error in Am1 is most likely the result of a slip, rather than reflective of more significant phenomena (see also below, cap. 4, uos for nos). illi deus in choreb chorebh chor?? choreb

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 1:9 dixique vobis illo in tempore

item.title:
Dixit moyses ad populum in tempore illo non potero solus sufferre fufferre sufficere uos et ordinaui ordina ordinaui ordinauit
uobis
uobis uobis
uiros
uiros sapientes qui praeessent prȩessent uobis

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Et tollentes se
se
de choreb decoribus As in some other chapters (cf. 9, 22, 114, 133), Am1 and Tu share an erroneous reading that may suggest a closer relationship between them; however, this is not to the only possible explanation (see under "Info"). choreb de choreb de horeb de horeb Φ, de Bruyne vs Am1, Tu (error) ambulauerunt per omnem heremum eremum Φ, Am1, Tu vs de Bruyne (orthography)

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Accessistis ad me et
et dixistis mittamus uiros ante nos uos The trivial error in Am1 is most likely the result of a common misreading, rather than reflective of more significant phenomena (see also above, cap. 1, praecipit for praecepit). et feci fecisti fecisti feci secundum
hoc uerbum

Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title: Et dixi dixit uobis dominus deus praecedit praecedet praecepit praecedet praecedit praecepit praecedit praecepit praecedit precedit uos et non credidistis credistis credidistis

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Murmurastis murmorastis murmurattis murmurastis et exacerbatus exercebatus exazerbatus est dominus et iurauit non ?n non
intraturos intraturus
uos in terram illam bonam nisi chaleb chabeb chaleb caleb et filios filius eius

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Et mihi michi iratus est dominus
causa uestri dicens nec
et
tu intrabis ibi
sed hiesus iesus Φ (praeter Tr P4), Tu vs Am1, de Bruyne (orthography) filius naue intrabit ibi et filii fili filii eorum quos dixerunt captiuos futuros

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 1:41 ascendemus atque pugnabimus (Am1: 1:41 cumque instructi)

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title: Et contra mandatum andictum interdictum In contrast with certain readings in other chapters (cf. 3, 22, 114, 133), the error in Am1 and Tu is here only partially comparable. Am1's andictum can easily be explained as a palaeographic error for mandatum (the sequence ic, in particular, must derive from the misreading of an open a). Tu's interdictum, by contrast, can likely be explained as a temptative emendation of a corrupt and ungrammatical form such as Am1's, and it also brings the capitulum, from a certain point of view, closer to the corresponding biblical passage (Dt 1:41-43). If this is correct, Tu may descend either from Am1 or from an ancestor of Am1 that already exhibited the error andictum. Φ, by contrast, cannot depend on this same ancestor, since it preserves the correct reading mandatum, which could not plausibly be restored (without contamination) from andictum. Φ vs Am1 (error) vs Tu (adiaph.) ascendistis in montem monte et exiuit amorraeus amorreus amorrȩus amorræus et persecutus est uos

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Similiter decurrit per singula et dinumerat uniuersa magnalia dei et uirtutes uirtutis quibus praeibat praedicabat
eos
eos deus et quomodo aluit a?luit aluit eos
deus
in deserto per
quadraginta xl l xl annos et quae quȩ fecit deus
deus duobus regibus seon et og

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title: Et precatus praecatus sum domino dominum domino dominum This is one of the cases in which the Turonian Bibles (or the majority of them) exhibit a reading that is morphologically or syntactically incorrect (cf. also cap. 25, 79, 90, 101, 118, 129, 130, 152); note that in this case (as in cap. 25, 79, 90), Am1 presents the same reading. Such readings may go back to an ancestor characterized by irregular linguistic forms, perhaps linked to a pre-Hieronymian version of Deuteronomy. It should be noted, however, that the only preserved witness to a Vetus Latina text of Deuteronomy (Codex Lugdunensis; VL 100) presents regular forms in all but one (cap. 101) of these instances. Φ, Am1 (error) vs Tu, deBruyne in tempore illo et despexit me uestri causa

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title: Hic hinc hic iterum astringit astrinxit adstringit adstrinxit astringit ne discedant a mandatis

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 4:15 non vidistis aliquam (rectius: 4:16 ne forte decepti)

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title: Videte ne faciatis uobis sculptile sculptiles Sculptile is to be understood as an adjective used substantively and can therefore be regarded as the correct reading. It reflects a Vetus Latina text, as suggested by the reading sculptilem in VL 100 (Dt 4:16: uidete ne execraueritis et faciatis ipsi uobis sculptilem similitudinem); the Vulgate instead reads sculptam (ibid.: ne forte decepti faciatis vobis sculptam similitudinem). similitudinem omnis hominis aut pecoris peccoris aut alicuius rei quae quæ sunt super terram aut in caelo cælo aut in mari

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Dominus iratus est mihi michi propter uos et non intrabo uobiscum sed morior
in hac ac hac terra adtendite attendite ne obliuiscamini testamentum domini dei uestri quoniam si exacerbaueritis acerbauerisis exacerbaueritis
deum dominum testor uobis hodie caelum cȩlum cælum et terram quia perietis perie?tis perietis de
terra illa

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Interrogate si a quo interroga tesiaquo
creauit creauerit deus hominem auditum est hoc si est gens
sub caelo cȩlo cælo cui locutus loquutus sit dominus deus de medio igni ignis Following the apparatus of the Benedictine edition of the Vulgate (vol. 3, p. 304; see bibliography), the reading ignis, preferred by de Bruyne, is attested only in the Bible of San Paolo fuori le Mura. It is therefore likely an innovation, and there is no reason to prefer it over the reading igni of the rest of the tradition. Φ, Am1, Tu vs de Bruyne (adiaph.)

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title: Tunc disposuit moyses ?oyses moyses ciuitates ad refugium homicidarum

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 4:44 ista est lex quam proposuit

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Haec hȩc hæc est lex quam locutus loquutus est moyses ??oyses moyses conuocans ad se
omnem israhel

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Haec hȩc
est uerba
quae
locutus loquutus est dominus
ad omnem
synagogam sinagogam israhel et dixistis mihi michi
ut et audirem a deo et locutus loquutus
sum
essem uobis et nunc non Φ, Tu, de Bruyne vs Am1 (error) facite quaecumque praecepit praecipit praecepit praecipit The manuscript tradition is almost equally divided between the perfect praecepit and the present praecipit. Preference has been given to the former, which coincides to the Vetus Latina version of the underlying passage (Dt 5:32) in the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100): Et obseruabitis ita facere sicut praecepit tibi Dominus Deus tuus. For a similar error see below, cap. 22. Φ (part), Tu, de Bruyne vs Φ (part), Am1 (adiaph.) uobis dominus dominus dominus uobis uobis dominus dominus uobis dominus non declinantes
in dexteram dextram dexteram dextram neque in sinistram

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 6:4 audi israhel dominus deus noster

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Audi israhel dominus deus noster dominus deus dominus unus est et diliges diligis diligens diliges The present tense found in Am1 is clearly inferior to the future, which is not only better suited to the context, but also matches the verb tense of the corresponding biblical passage (Dt 6:4-5). It is worth noting that diliges is the translation of the Vulgate, whereas the Vetus Latina (VL100) reads amabis. Φ, Tu, de Bruyne vs Am1 (error) dominum deum tuum ex toto corde tuo et
ex tota uirtute tua

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title: Dominum deum tuum timebis et illi soli seruies

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title: Non ibitis ibis post deos daeos deo alienos ne eradicemini

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Non temptabis temtabis dominum deum tuum sed facies quae quȩ
praecepit praecipit praecipit praecepit praecepit praecipit The perfect praecepit matches the text of the corresponding biblical passage (Dt 6:17), both in the Vetus Latina (seruando seruabis praecepta Domini Dei vestri, et testimonia, et iustitias quascumque praecepit tibi) and the Vulgate (custodi praecepta Domini Dei tui ac testimonia et caerimonias quas praecepit tibi). For a similar error see above, cap. 18. Φ (part), Tu, de Bruyne vs Φ (part), Am1
tibi et filio tuo dicesque dices De Bruyne's dices is likely the correct reading of the A series (and is in fact printed in the Benedictine edition of the Vulgate), both for reasons of internal consistency (since the second part of the capitulum is already introduced by an et, there is no need of a -que) and because it aligns with the Vetus Latina version of the corresponding passage (Dt 6:21, in VL100: et dices filio tuo...). The -que (which, however, is shared by all our witnesses) may have arisen from an erroneous word segmentation of the sequence dicesquemadmodum, as may be suggested by the reading admodum in Am1 Tu (see below). In this case, the ancestor of the Turonensian Bibles may have corrected by conjecture admodum to quemadmodum in order to restore the proper sense. Φ, Am1, Tu (error) vs de Bruyne quemadmodum admodum Together with some other readings in different chapters (cf. 3, 9, 114, 133), this shared error in Am1 and Tu may point to a closer relationship between these two pre-Turonian exemplars. In this case, though, a different explanation is also possible (see above the commentary to dicesque). Φ, de Bruyne vs Am1, Tu (error) eduxerit te dominus de aegypto ȩgypto ægypto

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Quod si introduxerit te dominus in terram quam possessurus es disperdes desperdes disperdas omnes gentes ipsas et aras eorum earum et titulos titulus titulos uitulos titutos titulos et sculptilia sculptibilia
combures conbures igni

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Si obseruaueritis praecepta et iustitias iusticias istas seruabit et
uobis dominus testamentum quod iurauit patribus uestris et benedicet uos uobis et expellet omnem languorem langorem a uobis

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Non parces parce? parces gentibus quas dominus subiciet subiciat Although not impossible, Am1's subjunctive is clearly inferior to the future found in all the other witnesses, which also matches the text of the biblical passage in question (Dt 7:16, daturus est tibi, in both VL 100 and the Vulgate). Φ, Tu, de Bruyne vs Am1 (error)
e tibi nec dices dicis The reading of Am1 is evidently inferior (all the verbs in the context are in the future) and is likely the result of a mere slip. Φ, Tu, de Bruyne vs Am1 (error) magna gens haec hȩc hæc et quomodo potero aduersus eam eum eam et non concupiscens cupiscens concupisces This is one of the cases in which the Turonian Bibles (or the majority of them) exhibit a reading that is morphologically or syntactically incorrect (cf. also chapters 11, 79, 90, 101, 118, 129, 130, 152); note that in this case both Am1 and Tu present the same reading. Such readings may go back to an ancestor characterized by irregular linguistic forms, perhaps linked to a pre-Hieronymian version of Deuteronomy. It should be noted, however, that the only preserved witness to a Vetus Latina text of Deuteronomy (Codex Lugdunensis; VL 100) presents regular forms in all but one (cap. 101) of these instances. Am1, Tu, Φ (error) vs deBruyne aurum uel argentum de sculptilibus eorum sed anathematizabis anatemazabis anatematizabis
ea eam

Omnia mandata quae quæ
trado tradidi uobis hodie obseruabitis ut et uiuatis et in mente habete habet omnia quae que quȩ experti estis in desertis desertus desertos desertis et esuristis
??????
et dedit
uobis dominus manna mannam Φ (praeter P4) vs Am1, Tu, de Bruyne ut sciretis quia non in pane pan?e pane tantum uiuit homo sed in omni uerbo dei

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 8:11 observa et cave ne quando

item.title: Adtende attende tibi ne obliuiscaris beneficia domini dei tui

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 8:17 ne diceres in corde tuo

item.title: Non ne dicas in corde tuo uirtus mea fecit haec hae omnia

Audi israhel tu transies hodie iordanen iordanem et possidebis gentes magnas et ciuitates ciuitatis ciuitates munitas et scies quia dominus deus tuus
praecedet te

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 9:4 ne dicas in corde tuo

item.title: Non dicas in corde tuo propter iustitias iniustitias iustitias iusticias meas introduxit me dominus in terram istam

In mente habete quotiens quoties quoties quotiens
exacerbaueritis exacerbaueris exaceruaueritis exacerba??tis exacerbastis exaceruaueris exaceruaueritis exaceruaueris exacerbaueritis exaceruaueris exacerbaueritis exaceruaueris exacerbaueris exacerbaueritis It is surprising that an apparently straightforward word like exacerbaueritis gave rise to so much variation within the manuscript tradition. dominum in desertis diffidentes deficientes The reading diffidentes reproduces the Vetus Latina version of the passage in question (Dt 9:7, in VL100: diffidentes consummabitis quae ad Dominum). Deficientes is more likely a slip (perhaps with a palaeographic basis) than the remnant of an older, unattested Latin translation. Its presence in P4 is not easily explained, as this manuscript usually has the standard Turonian text and shows otherwise only superficial agreement with Am1 (cf. chapters 26, 114, 129, 138). Φ (praeter P4), Tu, de Bruyne vs Am1, P4

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 9:8 nam et in horeb provocasti

item.title: Et in choreb exaceruastis exacerbastis dominum quando fecistis conflatile

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 9:12 dixitque mihi surge (rectius 9:13 rursumque ait)

item.title: Et dixit dominus ad me sine me et deleam illos uos eos Φ, Tu, de Bruyne vs Am1 (error)
Et descendi de monte et uidi quia peccastis et proieci progeci tabulas de manibus meis et confregi illas

Et deprecatus sum dominum
dominum iterum sicut prius quadraginta xl diebus et quadraginta xl noctibus panem non edi comedi edi et aquam non bibi
Et in aaron iratus est ualde dominus dominus ualde Φ, Am1, de Bruyne vs Tu (adiaph.) pro eo quod fecistis fecis?? fecistis uitulum

lem.title:
Et sumpsi et sumpsit sumpsi et
combussi conbussi combusi illud

app.note: The scribe of Am1 inverted capitula 37 and 38. A later, though still medieval, hand corrected the error by adding two small notes above both capitula.

lem.title: Et in temptatione exaceruastis exacerbastis dominum

app.note: The scribe of Am1 inverted capitula 37 and 38. A later, though still medieval, hand corrected the error by adding two small notes above both capitula.

item.title:
Et quando dimisit demisit misit Despite the widespread presence of the reading demisit in the manuscript tradition, since the error is trivial, I have preferred to retain dimisit, which aligns with the Vetus Latina text of the passage in question (Dt 9:23: Et cum dimisit uos Dominus...).
uos dominus dominus uos uos uos dominus
a in cades barne barnae

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 9:24 a die qua nosse vos (Am1, rectius: 9:25 et iacui coram domino)

item.title:
Et precatus praecatus prȩcatus sum ante dominum quadraginta xl
diebus qua diebus et quadraginta xl noctibus

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 10:1 in tempore illo dixit dominus

item.title:
In illo tempore dixit mihi dominus praecide decide According to the Vetus Latina text of the passage in question (Dt 10:1), the expected reading is decide, which appears in Am1, Tu and de Bruyne's edition; the Vulgate has dola. The reading praecide of the Tours Bibles probably derives from the Vulgate version of the parallel passage of Exodus, 34:1, which reads: Praecide, ait, tibi duas tabulas... Φ (adiaph.) vs Am1 Tu
tibi
tibi tib? tibi duas tabulas lapideas sicut priores et ascende ad in me in montem

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 10:3 feci igitur arcam (Am1: 10:3 cumque dolassem)

item.title: Et feci arcam et ascendi in montem
Et descendi discendi et misi tabulas in arcam

et In illo tempore separauit dominus
dominus deus
tribum tribu leui ut portarent arcam

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 10:12 et nunc israhel quid dominus

item.title:
Et nunc israhel quid dominus postulat a ad te aliud quam ut timeas dominum
deum tuum et ut diligas eum et seruias seruies ei
Dominum deum deum tuum timebis temebis et illi soli seruies

Cum septuaginta quinque lxxv lxx lxxv xlxxv animabus descenderunt patres tui nostri in
aegyptum ȩgyptum et fecit te
te deus sicut stellas caeli cæli cȩli in multitudinem multitudine Both readings, in multitudinem (as in the manuscripts) and in multitudine (as in de Bruyne's edition), seem acceptable, although the former probably gives a better sense. In view of their diffusion, the ablative being very rare (cf. also the apparatus of the Benedictine edition), preference can be given to the form with the accusative. Note, however, that neither has a perfect match in the text of VL 100, which reads nunc autem fecit te dominus deus tuus tamquam stelas caeli plenitudine (Dt 10:22). Φ (praeter L1), Am1, Tu vs de Bruyne, L1

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 11:1 ama itaque dominum deum tuum

item.title:
Et amabis dominum et scies quomodo te eduxit dominus de aegypto ȩgypto Due to the poor quality of the digitisation, the reading of Tr is uncertain. et quae fecerit dathan nathan et abiron habiron abir?n abiron
filiis filius
eliab heliab

Quod si audieritis praecepta illius dabit pluuiam terrae terrȩ
uestrae uestrȩ uestræ et satietatem satitatem satietatem sacietatem societatem in agris uestris

Et si obaudieritis omne omnem omne omnem praeceptum istud eiciet dominus omnes gentes omnem gentem The plural corresponds to the text of both the Vetus Latina and the Vulgate (Dt 11:23). Φ, Tu, de Bruyne vs Am1 (error)
a facie faciae uestra

Ecce icce ecce
do d??? do uobis hodie benedictiones et maledictiones
Ista sunt praecepta et iudicia iudicia et praecepta praecepta et iudicia quae obseruabitis
Non facietis uoluntatem uestram

In loco quem elegerit dominus adferetis adferetis afferetis afferetis auferetis
omnia The final letter of "afferetis" and the initial letter of "omnia" are no longer clearly visible due to a damp stain.
quae que que quae quȩ ego mando uobis

Omnes primitias primicias
tuas
in conspectu domini dei domin dei The copyist began to write domini a second time, but noticed the error in time to correct it by erasing the second minim of the n. tui manducabis
Adtendite attendite ne manducetis sanguinem

Custodi et fac omnia uerba
uerba
quae quæ ego mando tibi

Omne uerbum uerbo Φ, Tu, de Bruyne vs Am1 (error) quod ego mando tibi custodi
et fac et prophetam profetam propheta
qui dixerit
The copyist, believing that the capitulum ended with dixerit, initially wrote qui dixerit above the line, adjacent to the preceding capitulum (which is a common expedient that allows to economize space when transcribing a sequence of short lines). Recognising his mistake, he subsequently cancelled this qui dixerit and proceeded to copy the remainder of the capitulum on the next line.
qui quȩ
dixerit
tibi eamus et seruiamus diis non
audies audias The text from "qui dixerit" to the initial letter of "audies" is lost in L2 due to trimming of the lower margin.

Post dominum deum
t???
uestrum ibitis et ipsum timebitis

Si sin autem frater pater frater tuus aut filius aut coniux aut amicus dixerit tibi eamus seruiamus diis non parces ei sed eis et eis sed The singular is recommended by the context (cf. below eum) and is closer to the biblical text of the passage in question (Dt 13:8), both in the Vetus Latina (VL100: Non consenties ei neque exaudies eum, et non parcet oculus tuus super eum) and in the Vulgate (Non acquiescas ei, nec audias, neque parcat ei oculus tuus).
deferes defeceres deferes defferes eum et lapidabitur

app.witDetail: The fading of the red ink has rendered the letters "LX" hardly visible; the reading "LXI", however, remains certain.

item.title:
Si sin autem audieris aute eris in una ex ciuitatibus tuis tuus tuis esse homines peccatores peccatore peccatores
qui recedere
qui recedere fecerunt a deo omnes inhabitantes in ea et si uere factum fac est interficies omnes
Non facietis decaluationem decaluitione super mortuum numerum

Haec pecora quae quam
manducabitis manducabis Φ, Tu, de Bruyne vs Am1 (error)
Haec hæc manducabitis ab his quae sunt in aquis

Omnem auem autem
mundam mundum mundam mundum manducabitis

Decimas agri tui tuo
offeres offerres in loco quem quae est quem elegerit dominus

Cum autem longe fuerit a te uendes uindes
ea a ea et illic illi redimes

app.witDetail: Due to the poor quality of the digitisation, the reading is uncertain.

item.title: Post septem septe annos facies remissionem
Si audieris uocem domini dei tui princeps eris gentium multarum

Si
autem factus fuerit fueris in te egens de fratribus tuis

item.title:
Si sii si
emeris emeri emeris emeritis puerum hebraeum hebreum haebraeum
sex vi
annos annis seruiet tibi

item.title: Omne primitiuum primitium primitiuum primitium sanctificabis domino

item.title:
Custodi mensem mensam mensem nouorum et facies facias
pascha pasca domino deo tuo

item.title:
Iudices et scribas constitues constituas constitues contitues consties constituas constitues
ut et
iudicent iudicennt iuste

item.title:
Non
non plantabis tibi lucum lutum lucum lutum

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Si inuentus fuerit in te seruiens seruies seruiens diis in duobus uel tribus testibus
moriatur
moriatur
morietur moriatur In P8, the word moriatur was probably omitted by the first scribe and later restored above the line by a hand likely contemporaneous with, or slightly later than, the original; the restored word was subsequently erased.
Si latuerit te uerbum uade ua?de uade ad sacerdotem
Qui non audierit sacerdotem moriatur

Alienigena alienigenam alieniginam alienigena alienigenam This is one of the cases in which the Turonian Bibles (or the majority of them) exhibit a reading that is morphologically or syntactically incorrect (cf. also chapters 11, 25, 90, 101, 118, 129, 130, 152); note that in this case (as in cap. 11, 25, 90), Am1 presents the same reading. Such readings may go back to an ancestor characterized by irregular linguistic forms, perhaps linked to a pre-Hieronymian version of Deuteronomy. It should be noted, however, that the only preserved witness to a Vetus Latina text of Deuteronomy (VL 100) presents regular forms in all but one (cap. 101) of these instances. Φ (praeter Sg1 K), Am1 (error) vs Tu, deBruyne non facies facietis The plural form is inconsistent both with the context (cf. below te) and with the corresponding biblical passage (Dt 17:14-15). Φ, Tu, de Bruyne vs Am1 (error) principem super te
Hoc iudicium sacerdotum sacerdotem sacerdotem sacerdotum

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 18:9 quando ingressus fueris (rectius: 18:10 nec inveniatur)

item.title:
Non n non inuenietur in te lustrans lutrans lustrans
filium filiam filium suum aut filiam

app.note: The whole capitulum was omitted by the scribe of Sg1 and subsequently restored in the top margin by a contemporary or slightly later hand.

item.title: Propheta prophetam si locutus loquutus fuerit uerbum et non contigerit contingerit

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 19:3 et in tres (Am1, rectius: 19:4 haec erit lex homicidae)

item.title:
Hoc praeceptum preceptum
homicidae homicide homicidȩ homidae

Non transferes tranfferes transferes terminos quos quas
posuerant posuerunt Although shared by all our witnesses, the pluperfect is inconsistent with the text of the corresponding biblical passage (Dt 19:14), both in the Vetus Latina (VL 100: non transmouebis terminos proximi tui quos statuerunt priores tui in possessione tua) and in the Vulgate (non adsumes et transferes terminos proximi tui quos fixerunt priores in possessione tua). The use of the verb pono in the capitulum may reflect interference from a very close parallel in Proverbs 22:28 (Vulgate), Ne transgrediaris terminos antiquos, quos posuerunt patres tui. Notably, some patristic citations attest, for this verse, the existence of variants implying the verb transfero, as in our capitulum (and in the corresponding passage of the Vulgate), instead of transgredior. patres tui
Non accipies testimonium unius unus unius aduersus hominem hominum hominem
Animam anima pro anima animam oculum pro oculo dentem pro dente
Si exieris ad bellum noli noli?? noli timere

Si
??????
accesseris expugnare ciuitatem

Si obsideris obsideris obsederis obsederis This is one of the cases in which the Turonian Bibles (or the majority of them) exhibit a reading that is morphologically or syntactically incorrect (cf. also chapters 11, 25, 79, 101, 118, 129, 130, 152); note that in this case (as in cap. 11, 25, 79), Am1 presents the same reading. Such readings may go back to an ancestor characterized by irregular linguistic forms, perhaps linked to a pre-Hieronymian version of Deuteronomy. It should be noted, however, that the only preserved witness to a Vetus Latina text of Deuteronomy (VL 100) presents regular forms in all but one (cap. 101) of these instances. Φ, Am1 (error) vs Tu, de Bruyne ciuitatem
Si inuentus inuentum inuentus fuerit uulneratus in terra terram

Si tradiderit tradederit
tibi
deus inimicos tuos
Si fuerint homini duae duȩ uxores dilecta et dilectae odibilis

Si fuerit fuerint
alicui alicuius alicuius alicuiui alicuius is also the reading of the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100); the dative alicui, however, is closer to the Greek text of the LXX (τινι). Φ (praeter K), Am1, de Bruyne vs Tu (adiaph.) filius inoboediens inobediens
Si fuerit fuerint in aliquo alio peccatum ad mortem
Si uideris animal fratris tui errantem uel cadentem
Non induet inducet induet?? induet Φ, Tu, de Bruyne vs Am1 (error) mulier uestem uest?m uestem uirilem

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Si inueneris inueneneris
nidum nudum auium

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title: Si domum nouam aedificaueris aedificaberis ȩdificaueris

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title: Non arabis in uitulo et asino

Si quis acceperit accepit acceperit acciperit uxorem et odierit oderit oderit odierit odoerit odierit This is one of the cases in which the Turonian Bibles (or the majority of them) exhibit a reading that is morphologically or syntactically incorrect (cf. also chapters 11, 25, 79, 90, 118, 129, 130, 152). Notably, however, only here the same irregularity is found in the text of the corresponding biblical passage (Dt 22:13) in the Vetus Latina (VL100: Si autem quis acceperit uxorem et inhabitauerit cum ea et odierit eam); the Vulgate reads: Si duxerit vir uxorem, et postea odio habuerit eam. In all the other instances, VL100 presents regular forms. Φ (part; error) vs Φ (part), Am1, Tu, de Bruyne eam

Si inuentus fuerit adulter cum adultera mulierem The error may stem from a gloss to "adultera". This is not the only instance of an ablative in -m in Tu (cf. chapters 87, 103, 118); however, the presence of the -m may at times result from a slip caused by other surrounding accusatives, rather than from uncertain linguistic knowledge. Φ, Am1, de Bruyne vs Tu (error)

Si dormierit dormieris donmierit aliquis cum puella puellam
desponsata disponsata desponsatam This error ("puellam desponsatam") is not the only instance of an ablative in -m in Tu: cf. chapters 87, 103, 118.

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Si in campo dormierit in campo inuenerit quis puellam pullam
desponsatam disponsatam et uim fecerit ei

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title: Si inuenerit quis puellam uirginem et dormierit cum ea

Non accipiet homo uxorem fratris patris fratris patris The correct reading should be patris, in agreement with both the Vetus Latina and the Vulgate text of the passage in question (Dt 22:30: non accipiet homo uxorem patris sui). The reading fratris of the Turonian Bibles may be the result of a polar error. Φ (error) vs Am1, Tu, deBruyne sui

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

app.note: The trimming of the lower margin affected the text of all this title in P8, but the reading remains unambiguous.

app.witDetail: Due to the tight binding, the number is not visible in the digitisation.

item.title:
Non intrabit
abscisus
fornicaria nec ammanites amanites ammanita ammanites
et nec Φ, Tu, de Bruyne vs Am1 (adiaph.) moabites moabitas mol moabites moabita moabites in ecclesiam ecclesia ecclesias Φ, de Bruyne vs Am1, Tu (error) domini

item.title:
Non abominabis abhominabis anominabis abominabis abominab??s abominabis
idumaeum idumeam idumȩum idumeum neque aegyptium aegyptum

lem.title: Si fuerit homo inmundus immundus exibit extra castra

app.rdg: Si fuerit homo inmundus exibit extra castra
app.note: The scribe of Z inverted capitula 110 and 111. The rubricator corrected the error by adjusting the chapter numbers and adding two small signs to indicate that the two capitula should be transposed.

lem.title:
Non trades
tradas seruum domino suo

app.rdg: Si fuerit homo inmundus exibit extra castra

item.title:
Non erit israhelites israhel lites israhelitaes israhelitas israbelites israhelites
fornicaria fornicariae fornicariam
Non inferes mercedem fornicariae fornicariȩ

Non exiges exigens Together with some other readings in different chapters (cf. 3, 9, 22, 133), the shared error exigens in Am1 and Tu may point to a closer relationship between these two pre-Turonian exemplars. exies a fratre fratri tuo usuram
Si uoueris uotum domino non tardes trades tardes reddere illud

Si intraueris in
in
in uineam proximi tui

lem.title: Si intraueris in segetem proximi tui

app.note: The entire capitulum is omitted in de Bruyne's edition, likely because of a typographic error.

item.title:
Si quis acceperit acciperit acceperis uxorem et inuenerit in
in
ea eam This is not the only instance of an ablative in -m in Tu: cf. chapters 87, 102, 103. rem turpam turpam turpem turpem This is one of the cases in which the Turonian Bibles (or the majority of them) exhibit a reading that is morphologically or syntactically incorrect (cf. also chapters 11, 25, 79, 90, 101, 129, 130, 152). Such readings may go back to an ancestor characterized by irregular linguistic forms, perhaps linked to a pre-Hieronymian version of Deuteronomy. It should be noted, however, that the only preserved witness to a Vetus Latina text of Deuteronomy (Lyon, BM, 403 + 1964) presents regular forms in all but one (cap. 101) of these instances. Φ (error) vs Am1, Tu, deBruyne
Si quis acceperit uxorem in uxorem uxorem in recenti non exiet ad bellum
Non pignorabis pignerabis VL100 (Dt 24:6) reads pignerabis as Am1 and Tu (see also below, cap. 123). Φ vs Am1, Tu, de Bruyne (adiaph.) molam
Si captus fuerit fur
In mente habe quae quȩ fecerit dominus mariae mariæ

Debitorem non pignorabis pignerabis Pignerabis is closer to VL 100's text of the corresponding passage (Dt 24:10: non intrabis in domum ipsius pignerare pignus). See also above, cap. 120. Φ vs Am1, Tu, de Bruyne (adiaph.) per uim

In pignore pauperis pauperes non dormies et mercedem laboranti ne tarde tardi tardideris
reddideris
reddere
Non morientur patres pro filiis

Non declinabis iudicium in iuditio
aduenae aduenȩ aduene et orfani orphani et uiduae uiduæ uiduȩ
Non pignorabis uiduam
Si secaueris seccaueris segetem

Si liges leges liges colliges This is one of the cases in which the Turonian Bibles (or the majority of them) exhibit a reading that is morphologically or syntactically incorrect (cf. also chapters 11, 25, 79, 90, 101, 118, 130, 152). Such readings may go back to an ancestor characterized by irregular linguistic forms, perhaps linked to a pre-Hieronymian version of Deuteronomy. It should be noted, however, that the only preserved witness to a Vetus Latina text of Deuteronomy (Lyon, BM, 403 + 1964) presents regular forms in all but one (cap. 101) of these instances. Φ (praeter P4) (error) vs P4, Am1, Tu, de Bruyne oliuam

Si uindemiabis uinde?miabis uindemiabis
uinea uineam uinea uineam uinea uineam This is one of the cases in which the Turonian Bibles (or the majority of them) exhibit a reading that is morphologically or syntactically incorrect (cf. also chapters 11, 25, 79, 90, 101, 118, 129, 152). Such readings may go back to an ancestor characterized by irregular linguistic forms, perhaps linked to a pre-Hieronymian version of Deuteronomy. It should be noted, however, that the only preserved witness to a Vetus Latina text of Deuteronomy (VL 100) presents regular forms in all but one (cap. 101) of these instances. Φ (praeter Tr) (error) vs Tr, Am1, Tu, de Bruyne

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 24:22 et idcirco praecipio (Am1: 25:1 si fuerit causa)

item.title: Si iudicaueris impium dabis ei quadraginta xl flagellos
Non infrenabis infremabis bouem triturantem

Si mortuus fuerit quis habens uxorem sine filiis
Together with some other readings in different chapters (cf. 3, 9, 22, 114), this shared error in Am1 and Tu may point to a closer relationship between these two pre-Turonian exemplars. The presence of the detail sine filiis is certainly to be considered the correct text, as it is required by the context and aligns with the corresponding biblical passage (Dt 25:5). In terms of phrasing, however, the text of the capitulum is closer to the Vulgate (unus ex eis absque liberis mortuus fuerit) than to the Vetus Latina (VL100: mortuus fuerit unus eorum, semen autem non fuerit ei). Φ, de Bruyne vs Am1, Tu (error) accipiet eam
frater eius
Si noluerit homo accipere uxorem fratris sui

Si rixauerint rexauerint rixauerint duo homines hominis homines
in in inter se

Non n non erit tibi pondus iniquum et mensurae mensure mensura mensurȩ
duplices duplicis duplex
In mente habe quanta tibi fecerit amalech amalec

Cum introieritis introieris
in
in
terram terra quam dominus deus
deus
????
dabit ?abit dabit uobis
Et dixit moyses ad populum tacete et audite

Hi hi hii
stabunt stabant stabunt benedicere plebem et hi
hi hii hi hii maledicere
Maledictus omnis qui fecerit haec

Si custodieritis praecepta precepta praeceptum praecepta domini uenient super uos
The omission in de Bruyne's edition is likely a typographic error, as super uos is required by the context and aligns with the corresponding biblical passage (Dt 28:2), both in the Vetus Latina (VL 100: Et uenient super te omnes benedictiones istae) and the Vulgate (Venientque super te uniuersae benedictiones istae). benedictiones
omnes
Si non audieritis uenient in ??? in uos maledictiones istae istȩ istæ

Vocauit moyses omnes filios israhel et dixit dicit dic?? dixit ad eos
CXLIIII
scitis quanta fecerit dominus pharaoni propter uos

bibl.title: Deuteronomium 29:28 proiecitque in terram (rectius 29:29 abscondita Domino)

item.title:
Quae quȩ sunt absconsa deo adeo quae autem palam proalam
nobis uobis nobis
Prope est uerbum in ore tuo et in corde tuo
Ecce dedi ante oculos tuos uitam et in mortem
Et consummauit moyses omnia uerba ista

Et uocauit moyses iesum et dixit ei uiriliter age et
conualesce conualescite

Dixit dominus ad moysen moyse? moysen
tu dormies cum patribus tuis et fornicabitur plebs ples plebs plebis
haec hȩc hæc
post deos alienos
Scripsit scribsit moyses canticum hoc

Dixit dominus ad moysen ascende in montem abarim et morire morere morire morere This is one of the cases in which the Turonian Bibles (or the majority of them) exhibit a reading that is morphologically or syntactically incorrect (cf. also chapters 11, 25, 79, 90, 101, 118, 129, 130). Such readings may go back to an ancestor characterized by irregular linguistic forms, perhaps linked to a pre-Hieronymian version of Deuteronomy. It should be noted, however, that the only preserved witness to a Vetus Latina text of Deuteronomy (Lyon, BM, 403 + 1964) presents regular forms in all but one (cap. 101) of these instances. Φ (error) vs Am1, Tu, de Bruyne ibi
Benedixit moyses homo dei filios israhel

Mortuus est
est moyses in terra terram moab

Et non fuit
iam
propheta prof propheta
in israhel The trimming of the lower margin damaged these words, although they remain legible.
sicut
Trimming of the lower margin caused the loss of the final part of this capitulum in P8.
fuit
Trimming of the lower margin caused the loss of the final part of this capitulum in P8.
moyses
Trimming of the lower margin caused the loss of the final part of this capitulum in P8.

rdgGrp.rdg: EXPLICUERUNT CAPituLa IN LIBRO DEUTERO NOMII
